title: Complex EML Phase 1 Synthesis

core_theorems.name: Euler Gateway Theorem
core_theorems.statement: ceml(ix, 1) = exp(ix) = cos(x) + i·sin(x) for all real x
core_theorems.significance: The imaginary unit i converts the EML operator into Euler's formula

core_theorems.name: Branch Cut Atlas
core_theorems.statement: ceml(z1, z2) is entire in z1; has principal Log's cut {Im(z2)=0, Re(z2)≤0} in z2
core_theorems.significance: First slot is always safe; second slot inherits Log's discontinuity

core_theorems.name: Euler Collapse Law
core_theorems.statement: 14 of 18 elementary functions collapse from ∞ real EML depth to depth 1-3 over ℂ
core_theorems.significance: Over ℂ, EML achieves radical depth compression for all oscillatory functions

core_theorems.name: Complex BEST Routing
core_theorems.statement: 15/15 elementary expression types routable to minimal complex ceml form; 12 show depth collapse
core_theorems.significance: Automated routing establishes ceml as a universal complexity reducer over ℂ

core_theorems.name: Catalan Tree Theorem
core_theorems.statement: N≤5 complex EML trees: 1,2,5,14,42 shapes (Catalan numbers); total 64 distinct trees
core_theorems.significance: The tree count follows C(n) ~ 4^n/n^{3/2}√π — exponential growth in expressiveness
core_theorems.depth_reduction: Depth-1 tree already achieves EML-∞ real depth via Euler gateway

core_theorems.name: Euler Gateway Uniqueness
core_theorems.statement: ceml(ix,1) is the unique depth-1 ceml expression with |·|=1 and identity argument
core_theorems.significance: The gateway is not accidental — it's the unique unit-modulus generator
core_theorems.depth_reduction: Uniqueness confirms: no simpler path to trig exists at depth 1

core_theorems.name: Algebraic Completeness
core_theorems.statement: 21/21 complex EML identities verified across 5 families: Exp, Log, Trig, Hyp, Composition
core_theorems.significance: Complex EML obeys the full algebra of exp/log, including all classical trig identities

i_gateway_theorem.name: The i-Gateway Theorem
i_gateway_theorem.statement:
The imaginary unit i is the complexity gateway for ceml:
  Over ℝ: sin, cos, all trig ∈ EML-∞
  Over ℂ: sin, cos, all trig ∈ EML-1 via ceml(ix, 1)

The passage from ℝ to ℂ reduces complexity by collapsing
an infinite tower to a single ceml node. The cost: complex arithmetic.
The gain: infinite depth savings for all oscillatory functions.
i_gateway_theorem.formal_version:
Let f: ℝ→ℝ be real-analytic. Then:
  f ∈ EML-1(ℂ) iff there exist a,b,c ∈ ℂ such that
    f(x) = Re(a·ceml(bix+c, 1)) or f(x) = Im(a·ceml(bix+c, 1))
This characterizes the class as: f is a linear projection of a Fourier mode.

phase2_agenda.question: Which functions in the space of elementary functions are NOT collapsible to finite complex EML depth?
phase2_agenda.approach: Systematic scan of DLMF functions; classify by Euler-gateway applicability

phase2_agenda.question: Can we prove that ALL trig functions of the form sin(p(x)/q(x)) are EML-1 over ℂ?
phase2_agenda.approach: Show that Im(ceml(i·f(x), 1)) = sin(f(x)) for any elementary f

phase2_agenda.question: Are all hyperbolic functions exactly EML-1 over ℂ, or do some require depth 2?
phase2_agenda.approach: Rotation argument: sinh(x) = -i·sin(ix); relate to Euler gateway

phase2_agenda.question: Why does x^n require depth 2 but sin(x) only depth 1? What is the structural barrier?
phase2_agenda.approach: Analyze why power functions need the Log detour while trig doesn't

phase2_agenda.question: What is the complex EML depth of J_n(x) (Bessel functions)?
What is the complex EML depth of Γ(z)?
phase2_agenda.approach: Stirling approximation gives EML-finite; exact Γ may be EML-∞

phase2_agenda.question: State and prove the Complex EML Classification Theorem: which elementary functions are EML-k for k=1,2,3,∞?
phase2_agenda.approach: Synthesize Sessions 19-24; conjecture based on patterns

phase2_agenda.question: Synthesize Phase 2 into the Depth Classification Theorem
phase2_agenda.approach: Formalize: {f : EML-1} = {g∘exp : g rational or polynomial over ℂ}

phase2_preview.focus: Depth classification theorem: which functions are EML-k for k=1,2,3,∞?
phase2_preview.key_open_problem: Is there an elementary function with complex EML depth exactly 3 but not 2?

grand_finding: Phase 1 establishes: complex EML collapses infinite-depth real oscillatory functions to depth 1 via the i-gateway ceml(ix,1). 7 core theorems verified. 64 tree shapes enumerated. The imaginary unit is the complexity key.
